subject: EU Law
lesson_title: The European Parliament and the Commission: Supranational Institutions

questions.question: The European Parliament is composed of representatives elected by:

options.A: The national parliaments of member states
options.B: Citizens of the Union directly by universal suffrage
options.C: The governments of member states in proportion to population
options.D: The Commission, from lists provided by national political parties

questions.explanation: Article 14(3) TEU: the Members of the European Parliament (MEPs) shall be elected by direct universal suffrage in a free and secret ballot. Direct elections have been held since 1979, making the Parliament the only directly elected EU institution.

questions.question: The maximum number of MEPs is fixed by the Lisbon Treaty at:
questions.explanation: Article 14(2) TEU: the European Parliament shall not exceed 751 members (750 plus the President). Seats are allocated among member states on a principle of 'degressive proportionality' — smaller states get proportionally more seats per capita than larger states.

questions.question: The European Parliament exercises which of the following powers?

options.A: The exclusive right to propose legislation
options.B: Legislative and budgetary functions jointly with the Council, democratic scrutiny, and the power to censure the Commission
options.C: The power to dismiss individual Commissioners
options.D: The power to annul acts of the Council acting alone

questions.explanation: Article 14(1) TEU: the Parliament exercises, jointly with the Council, legislative and budgetary functions; it carries out functions of political control and consultation; and it elects the President of the Commission. Crucially, the Parliament can pass a motion of censure against the Commission as a whole (Article 234 TFEU), but cannot dismiss individual Commissioners.

questions.question: The European Commission consists of:

options.B: A number of members, including its President and High Representative, corresponding to two-thirds of the number of member states, unless the European Council decides otherwise
options.C: Twenty Commissioners appointed by QMV in the Council
options.D: One Commissioner per member state plus the President

questions.explanation: Article 17(5) TEU: from 2014, the Commission was to consist of members corresponding to two-thirds of the number of member states (unless the European Council, acting unanimously, decided otherwise). In practice, the European Council has continued to appoint one Commissioner per member state.

questions.question: The Commission has the exclusive right of legislative initiative in most areas. This means:

options.A: The Commission enacts legislation without Council or Parliament approval
options.B: The Council and Parliament can only legislate on a proposal from the Commission in most areas
options.C: The Commission can veto any legislation proposed by the Council
options.D: National parliaments must obtain Commission approval before introducing domestic legislation

questions.explanation: Article 17(2) TEU: Union legislative acts may only be adopted on the basis of a Commission proposal, except where the Treaties provide otherwise. This 'monopoly of initiative' makes the Commission the gatekeeper of the legislative agenda, though the Parliament and Council can request Commission proposals.

questions.question: The President of the Commission is appointed by which procedure?

options.A: Directly elected by EU citizens in a Union-wide election
options.B: Proposed by the European Council by QMV (taking into account Parliament elections), then elected by the Parliament by majority
options.C: Appointed by the Council of the EU by unanimity
options.D: Selected by a committee of heads of state acting by consensus

questions.explanation: Article 17(7) TEU: the European Council, acting by qualified majority and taking into account Parliament elections, proposes a candidate. The European Parliament then elects the candidate by majority. This 'Spitzenkandidat' process has created political tension about how closely Parliament's election results must guide the European Council's choice.

questions.question: The European Parliament's power of 'censure' under Article 234 TFEU means:

options.A: The Parliament can annul any Commission Decision it considers politically objectionable
options.B: If a motion of censure is adopted by a two-thirds majority of votes cast representing a majority of component MEPs, all Commissioners must resign collectively
options.C: The Parliament can remove individual Commissioners for misconduct
options.D: The Parliament can suspend Commission legislative proposals pending review

questions.explanation: Article 234 TFEU: the motion of censure requires a two-thirds majority of votes cast, representing a majority of the component members of the Parliament. If adopted, the Commission as a body must resign — including the President. The Parliament cannot target individual Commissioners.

questions.question: The Commission's role as 'Guardian of the Treaties' refers to its power to:

options.A: Amend the Treaties when member states breach their obligations
options.B: Bring infringement proceedings against member states under Articles 258-259 TFEU for failure to comply with EU law
options.C: Invalidate national legislation that conflicts with EU law
options.D: Impose fines on EU citizens who violate directly effective EU law

questions.explanation: The Commission has the primary responsibility for ensuring member states comply with EU law obligations. Under Article 258 TFEU, it can bring infringement proceedings before the Court of Justice. Under Article 260 TFEU, if a member state fails to comply with a Court judgment, the Commission can seek financial penalties.

questions.question: MEPs sit in the European Parliament in:

options.A: National delegations according to their member state
options.B: European political groups according to political affiliation, not national delegations
options.C: Subject matter committees, meeting permanently in Brussels

questions.explanation: MEPs do not sit in national blocks — they sit in European political groups (e.g., the European People's Party, the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats, Renew Europe). This structure is designed to promote transnational political alignment over national interests.

questions.question: The 'ordinary legislative procedure' makes the European Parliament:

options.A: A purely advisory body that the Council may consult but is not bound by
options.B: A co-legislator with the Council — legislation requires agreement of both institutions
options.C: Superior to the Council in the legislative process
options.D: Responsible only for budgetary decisions, not legislative ones

questions.explanation: Under the ordinary legislative procedure (Article 294 TFEU), legislation requires agreement of both the European Parliament (by majority) and the Council (typically by QMV). Either institution can block legislation, making them co-legislators with equal formal power over most EU legislative acts.

questions.question: The principle that Commissioners must act in the general interest of the Union means:

options.A: Commissioners may take instructions from their home member state government
options.B: Commissioners must be completely independent and neither seek nor take instructions from any government, institution, or other body
options.C: Commissioners must represent the interests of all member states equally
options.D: Commissioners must coordinate their policies with the Council Presidency

questions.explanation: Article 17(3) TEU: Commissioners must be chosen for their general competence and European commitment and be completely independent. They shall neither seek nor take instructions from any government, body or institution. Member states undertake to respect this principle and not to seek to influence Commissioners.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the European Parliament's role in the EU budgetary process?

options.A: The Parliament has no role — the Council alone adopts the EU budget
options.B: The Parliament jointly adopts the annual budget with the Council under a special procedure
options.C: The Parliament can propose expenditure but cannot reject the budget
options.D: The Parliament approves broad budgetary guidelines but the Commission determines specific allocations

questions.explanation: The EU budget is adopted jointly by the Parliament and Council under Article 314 TFEU. The Parliament has the power to reject the budget in its entirety (a power used in practice, e.g., in 1979 and 1984). The Parliament also grants discharge to the Commission for budget implementation under Article 319 TFEU.

questions.question: The resignation of the entire Santer Commission in 1999 was significant because:

options.A: It was the first time the Parliament exercised its censure power successfully
options.B: It demonstrated the Parliament's growing power — though the formal censure motion failed, the threat of censure caused the collective resignation following fraud and mismanagement findings
options.C: It was caused by the Court of Justice finding the Commission had violated fundamental rights
options.D: It was triggered by member states withdrawing their Commissioners en masse

questions.explanation: While the formal censure motion did not reach the required two-thirds threshold, the Parliamentary committee's devastating report on fraud, nepotism, and mismanagement left the Commission's position untenable. The Commission resigned collectively in March 1999, demonstrating the Parliament's power through political pressure even without formal censure — a landmark in Parliament's political development.
